notes.note: Calumnia was the offence of bringing a malicious or vexatious prosecution; a prosecutor convicted of it was branded with infamia. Sempronius's defeat in his own suit amounted to a calumnia-verdict against him.

notes.anchor: under the Lex Plotia on violence
notes.note: The lex Plotia (or Plautia) de vi established the standing criminal court for public violence (vis); cases under it took precedence, which is why Sempronius used it to manufacture a trial that would crowd out his own.
The subsellia were the benches in the law-court where parties, advocates and supporters sat; Caelius means he rushed over uninvited to join the defence of Tuccius and attack Sempronius.
A charge de repetundis, before the standing court for provincial extortion (the quaestio repetundarum), the chief instrument for calling former governors and officials to account.

notes.anchor: sworn the oath against vexatious accusation
notes.note: Before two rivals could compete for the right to prosecute (the divinatio), each swore he was not acting in calumnia; Appius the younger, having sworn, dared not press his claim against Pilius.

The temple of Apollo (in the Campus Martius, near the Circus Flaminius) was one of the meeting-places where the senate could lawfully convene outside the pomerium; the decree was drafted there on 30 September 51 BC.

A tribune of the plebs could veto (intercessio) any senatorial decree, reducing it to a mere auctoritas (recorded resolution); C. Caelius and his colleagues blocked the moves against Caesar in just this way.
